content:
Elastic found pieces of a malicious payload concealed inside SVG flag files, but simply viewing them was not the trigger in the infection chain it documented.

9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by ThisisEngineering on UnsplashYes, attackers can still conceal malicious payload data inside image files. But that does not necessarily mean the image itself can infect someone when viewed.
In a recent fake coding assessment, attackers used several SVG country-flag files to store fragments of a malicious payload.
The SVG files did not execute the malware on their own. Separate JavaScript inside the project had to read the SVG files, extract the concealed fragments, reconstruct the payload, decode it and deliberately execute it.
The more important issue was therefore not the SVG format alone.
It was a functional but trojanized software project, delivered through a convincing recruitment process and designed to activate through familiar development commands.
What Elastic discovered
On 18 July 2026, Elastic Security Labs disclosed a campaign it tracks as REF9403.
The investigation began after someone posted what appeared to be a developer opportunity in Elastic’s community Slack workspace. Interested users were then contacted privately and directed to complete a coding assessment as part of the supposed recruitment process.
The assessment was not an obviously broken or empty repository.
It contained a functional Next.js e-commerce application based on a legitimate open-source project template. The application could operate normally, but malicious additions had been placed among its ordinary project files.
That detail matters.
A developer trying to understand the application, fix a feature or complete an assessment may concentrate on whether the project builds and works correctly.
If the storefront loads successfully, the repository may appear legitimate enough to reduce suspicion.
Meanwhile, malicious behaviour can run quietly through server-side code that the candidate may not have reviewed closely.
How the malware was concealed inside the images
SVG, or Scalable Vector Graphics, is a text-based image format.
Unlike image formats that mainly store a grid of pixels, an SVG file contains readable markup describing shapes, colours, paths and other visual elements.
It can also contain comments that do not appear as part of the visible image.
In REF9403, the attackers placed Base64-encoded payload fragments inside comment blocks within multiple SVG files representing country flags.
Base64 is a common encoding used to represent binary data using text characters. It has many legitimate uses and is not automatically suspicious.
In this case, however, it helped conceal pieces of the payload inside files that appeared to be ordinary image assets.
The data was not hidden by manipulating visible pixels, changing the colours or drawing secret content into the flags.
Someone looking at the images would simply see normal country flags.
The concealed material existed within the text structure of the SVG files.
One file alone did not contain the complete payload. The data had been divided across several SVG files.
A JavaScript file named serverValidation.js then:

• Read the SVG files from the flags directory.

• Sorted them alphabetically.

• Extracted the concealed content from the comment blocks.

• Joined the fragments together.

• Decoded the reconstructed data.

• Executed the resulting JavaScript.
Elastic found that the project’s server code called this function during startup.
Both npm run dev and npm start launched the affected server component, causing the payload to be reconstructed and executed when the application server started.
So, could merely viewing the SVG infect someone?
Based on the execution chain Elastic documented, no.
Elastic’s report does not describe any of the following as the execution trigger:

• seeing the country flags in the application

• opening an SVG file in an image viewer

• viewing the file in a browser

• downloading the repository

• cloning the repository without running its code
The SVG files acted as containers for concealed data.
The dangerous behaviour depended on separate malicious JavaScript inside the project. That code had to read the files, recover the fragments, decode the reconstructed content and deliberately pass it to an execution function.
This is different from an image-viewing exploit.
In an image-viewing exploit, a vulnerability in a browser, image library or application may be triggered when it processes a specially crafted image.
Elastic did not describe that kind of vulnerability in REF9403.
The SVG files stored the concealed material. The project’s JavaScript provided the execution mechanism.
That does not make the use of SVG files harmless.
It shows how attackers can place dangerous components inside project assets that developers may be less likely to inspect.
Why a functional project can still be malicious
Developers are often expected to download and run unfamiliar code during technical interviews, open-source work and normal development tasks.
That normality is exactly what can make this approach convincing.
A candidate may expect to install dependencies, start a development server and troubleshoot an unfamiliar application. There may also be time pressure to complete the assessment and demonstrate technical ability.
If the project is professionally presented and works as expected, attention naturally moves towards completing the task rather than auditing every startup script, server-side file and project asset.
The malicious additions in REF9403 were also placed among legitimate components.
Country-flag assets would not necessarily look out of place in an e-commerce project, making the files easier to overlook.
What the malware could do
Elastic described the malware as OTTERCOOKIE-aligned based on technical and behavioural similarities with previously documented activity.
The researchers also observed growing overlap between OTTERCOOKIE and related malware such as BEAVERTAIL.
Capabilities that had previously been associated with different malware families appeared together in the collected samples, making strict family labels more difficult.
The malware included four main components:

• a browser credential and cryptocurrency-wallet data stealer

• a file-stealing component

• a Socket.IO-based remote-access backdoor

• a clipboard-stealing component that could also attempt to download additional executables on Windows
The browser-stealing component targeted saved credentials, autofill data and information belonging to cryptocurrency-wallet extensions.
The file-stealing component searched for material commonly found on developer systems, including:

• environment and configuration files

• SSH material

• cloud-related directories

• shell histories

• documents and spreadsheets

• source code

• other potentially sensitive local files
The remote-access component could connect to attacker-controlled infrastructure and receive commands, giving the operator a way to interact with the affected system.
Elastic also observed clipboard monitoring on Windows and macOS, although the implementation differed between operating systems.
The Windows sample used PowerShell to retrieve clipboard contents, while the macOS version used the system’s pbpaste utility.
The Linux sample examined by Elastic did not contain the same implemented clipboard-collection behaviour.
Elastic reported that the collected repositories had zero antivirus detections at the time of writing.
That does not mean they remain undetected or that security products are inherently unable to identify similar activity.
Detection can change as samples are analysed, indicators are shared and security tools are updated.
Why Elastic linked it to Contagious Interview
Elastic assessed REF9403 as DPRK-aligned activity related to the campaign commonly known as Contagious Interview. The delivered malware also shared technical and behavioural similarities with OTTERCOOKIE.
The assessment was based on technical connections involving:

• code similarities

• malware behaviour

• matching strings and script structures

• API endpoints

• related command-and-control infrastructure

• overlap with earlier public research
The attribution is based on technical overlap with previously documented activity.
Elastic’s public report does not identify every individual involved in distributing the repositories.
The wider fake-interview campaign
REF9403 forms part of a broader pattern.

Get Sayyidi Shaarani’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.
Remember me for faster sign in

Microsoft and GitLab have separately documented Contagious Interview activity in which threat actors pose as recruiters, employers or potential clients before directing developers towards malicious coding assignments.
Other documented cases have involved:

• malicious repositories and software packages

• commands presented as technical fixes

• dependency installation

• project startup scripts

• IDE task configurations and repository trust prompts
These techniques should not be mixed together as though all of them appeared in REF9403.
For this specific Elastic case, the documented execution chain was:

• A fake developer opportunity led to a coding assessment.

• The assessment contained a functional but trojanized project.

• Malicious server-side JavaScript read fragments concealed inside SVG files.

• The fragments were reconstructed and decoded.

• The resulting payload was executed when the server component started.
Another Contagious Interview repository may use a different activation method.
Why developers are valuable targets
A developer’s computer may contain considerably more than source code.
Depending on the role and environment, it may have access to:

• private source-code repositories

• cloud environments

• CI/CD systems

• SSH keys

• API tokens and credentials

• signing keys

• browser credentials

• password-manager data

• cryptocurrency wallets

• production infrastructure
Compromising one developer device may create opportunities for credential theft, account takeover, unauthorised repository access, lateral movement or a wider software-supply-chain compromise.
These are possible downstream risks, not confirmed outcomes of every REF9403 infection.
Elastic described developer compromise as a potential route towards wider organisational impact.
However, the public research does not establish that every person who ran one of the repositories caused a broader company or supply-chain compromise.
Before running an unfamiliar coding assessment
It may not be practical to audit every line of an unfamiliar application.
A few checks can still reduce the risk considerably.
Verify the opportunity separately
Do not rely only on the account that contacted you.
Check the company’s official website, careers page and independently verified contact channels.
Review whether the recruiter’s identity, email domain, employment history and job description are consistent.
Be cautious when a supposed recruiter quickly moves the conversation into private messages or provides an assessment that cannot be verified through the company.
Review what will execute
Before installing or starting the project, examine areas that commonly control execution:

• package.json scripts

• installation and post-installation hooks

• server startup files

• build scripts

• IDE configuration and task files

• unfamiliar dependencies

• code that downloads or executes remote content
Understand what will run during dependency installation, development startup, production startup and IDE workspace loading.
A familiar command can still trigger unfamiliar code.
In REF9403, normal project commands launched the server component that reconstructed and executed the concealed payload.
Encoded, compressed or minified data is not automatically malicious.
